title: Minor-ports promotion queue — sim-ready staging for PLAN-3 Phase S2
note: The 33 minor ports (research/minor-ports-1500-1830.json) — plus Istanbul, added at PLAN-3 R1 as the declared-boundary exemplar (34 total; tranches 12/12/10) — authored into promotable sim definitions. NOT loaded by the game: a port moves from here into data-src/ports.json + routes.json only when Phase S2 bakes its wind/current route fields (16 fields per port). The supporting vocabulary (cargo, powers/flags, junk & dhow rigs, regions) is ALREADY in data-src/ — promotion is: copy the port, expand its lanes into directed legs, bake, done. Tranches order by diversity-value ÷ bake-cost: 1 = bake first, 2 = next, 3 = has an open bake/design risk noted in bakeRisk/flagNeeded. Coordinates are approximate harbour positions for the 1° routing grid.
laneSchema: lanes[].with names the counterpart port (sim port id or staged id); out = cargo carried FROM this port TO the counterpart, home = the return leg; each pair expands to two directed routes.json legs at promotion.

ports.name: Naha (Ryukyu)
ports.significance: The licensed tribute conduit between China and Japan — a tiny kingdom made an entrepôt by uniquely favourable Ming terms.

ports.name: Macau
ports.significance: Portugal's toehold in China; carried the great silk-for-silver run to Nagasaki until 1639.

lanes.note: The nau do trato — ends with the Portuguese expulsion from Japan.

ports.name: Manila
ports.significance: Where American silver met Chinese silk — the western terminus of the Manila galleon.
ports.note: The Acapulco galleon itself runs east across the Pacific — Acapulco (−99.9°) sits just inside the map bounds; a candidate lane once Pacific field coverage is verified.

ports.name: Mocha
ports.significance: The Red Sea coffee staple that supplied Europe before Java — every company kept a factory here.

ports.name: Muscat
ports.significance: Seat of the Omani maritime empire that drove Portugal from the Swahili coast.

ports.name: Banda Neira
ports.significance: The world's only nutmeg source until the 1770s; the VOC monopoly at its most absolute — and most brutal (the 1621 conquest).

ports.name: Makassar
ports.significance: Gowa's free-trade entrepôt — the hole in the VOC spice monopoly, until the city fell in 1669.

ports.name: Elmina
ports.significance: 'The mine' — the Gold Coast castle trade, Portuguese then Dutch after 1637; gold first, later a major slave-trade factory (sober framing required for any such lane).

ports.name: Saint-Louis (Senegal)
ports.significance: The Senegal river trade — gum arabic, indispensable to Europe's textile printers.

ports.name: St Eustatius
ports.significance: The 'Golden Rock' — the Caribbean's free-port contraband entrepôt, so useful to the American rebels that Rodney sacked it in 1781.

ports.name: Portobelo
ports.significance: The isthmus fair where Potosí silver met the galeones — the treasure-fleet system's Caribbean hinge until 1739.

lanes.note: The galeones de Tierra Firme; the fair system dies with Vernon's raid and the register ships.

ports.name: Louisbourg
ports.significance: France's fortress fishery — dried cod for Catholic Europe, twice besieged, razed 1758.

lanes.note: The classic cod-for-rum triangle with the French islands (Kingston as proxy).

ports.name: Busan (Waegwan)
ports.significance: Joseon Korea's single licensed foreign window — the Japan House, worked by Tsushima-domain vessels.
ports.flagNeeded: tsushima — the licensed Japanese carrier; add as a small spawning flag at promotion (Korea itself spawned no foreign-going ships).

lanes.note: Proxy pairing: the real counterpart is Tsushima, not Nagasaki — decide at promotion.

ports.name: Hội An
ports.significance: The Nguyễn lords' licensed junk fair — silk, ceramics, aloeswood; Japanese and Chinese quarters side by side.
ports.flagNeeded: china-junk-trade — a spawnable flag for the private Chinese junk diaspora (distinct from the Qing state, which is a non-spawning shore power). Also unlocks Manila, Patani, Ayutthaya junk lanes.

ports.name: Galle
ports.significance: Ceylon cinnamon under VOC monopoly — the island's fortified export port.

ports.name: Malé
ports.significance: The cowrie fishery — shell money for Bengal and, through European hands, the West African trade.

lanes.note: European country-trade carriage (DAC as the Coromandel proxy); local and Indian bottoms carried it earlier — see flagNeeded on hoi-an for the country-trade flag question.

ports.name: Bandar Abbas
ports.significance: Safavid Persia's designated port after Hormuz fell — silk out, everything in; EIC and VOC factories side by side.

ports.name: Masulipatnam
ports.significance: Golconda's port — the painted-cotton (chintz) trade that dressed the world.
ports.powerNeeded: golconda — Qutb Shahi sultanate (to 1687), then Mughal; add as a non-spawning shore power at promotion.

ports.name: Calicut
ports.significance: The Zamorin's pepper port — two centuries of resistance to the Portuguese monopoly.

ports.name: Smyrna (İzmir)
ports.significance: The Levant trade's great port — Persian silk, Angora mohair, cotton and figs for the Levant Company and the French échelles.

lanes.note: The Levant Company's Smyrna fleet — Tourville burned one such convoy off Lagos in 1693.

ports.name: Ragusa (Dubrovnik)
ports.significance: The neutral Adriatic republic whose argosies carried for everyone — under Ottoman protection, in everyone's ports.

ports.name: Windau (Ventspils)
ports.significance: Duke Jacob's improbable fleet — a Baltic duchy with Tobago and Gambia colonies for a generation.

ports.name: Emden
ports.significance: The Brandenburg African Company's brief Gold Coast venture — Prussia's first blue-water fling.

lanes.note: Historically to Großfriedrichsburg, 50 km west of Elmina — same coast cell class.

ports.name: Old Calabar
ports.significance: The Efik merchant houses of the Bight of Biafra — a major embarkation point of the Atlantic slave trade, run on Efik terms; palm oil rises as abolition closes the trade.

lanes.note: PROMOTION RULE: the outward human cargo MUST replicate the Whydah pattern exactly — a middlePassage:true leg to a plantation colony carrying only enslaved-people, with the sober framing block. Never author it as ordinary cargo.

ports.name: Arkhangelsk
ports.significance: Russia's White Sea door to the west — Muscovy Company furs, tar and naval stores, eclipsed but never closed by Petersburg.
ports.bakeRisk: The White Sea approach rounds North Cape at ~71°N — above the engine's 66°N Arctic ice-cap seal. Needs a seasonal (summer-only) mask exception or a hand-carved corridor; decide before baking.

ports.name: Ayutthaya
ports.significance: Siam's cosmopolitan river capital — crown junks to Japan and China, deerskins and sappanwood, until the Burmese sack of 1767.
ports.bakeRisk: A river port ~90 km up the Chao Phraya — invisible to a 1° ocean grid. Stage the port at the river bar (coordinates given); the label can still read Ayutthaya.

ports.name: Patani
ports.significance: The queens of Patani ran the China–Malay entrepôt where the first Dutch and English factories in Siamese orbit opened.
ports.powerNeeded: patani — the sultanate under its four queens; add as a spawning or shore flag at promotion.

ports.name: Kharg Island
ports.significance: The VOC's last Gulf venture — a fortified pearl and transit station, thirteen years from founding to Mir Muhanna's sack.
ports.bakeRisk: Far up the Gulf on a 1° grid — verify the Hormuz channel routes cleanly before baking.

ports.name: Mombasa
ports.significance: Fort Jesus and the Swahili-coast ivory trade — Portuguese, then Omani after the great siege of 1698.
ports.bakeRisk: None structural; low priority only because the Indian-Ocean lanes above cover the region's cargo story first.

ports.name: Île Sainte-Marie
ports.significance: The pirate republic off Madagascar — Every, Kidd and the Indian-Ocean roundsmen careened here between the Pilgrim-fleet raids.
ports.powerNeeded: freebooters — a non-national flag; better modelled as a HAZARD (a regional riskUplift on Indian-Ocean lanes 1690–1725, like a war entry) than as a trade port. Decide at promotion; no lanes staged.

ports.name: Smeerenburg
ports.significance: 'Blubber town' on Spitsbergen — the Noordsche Compagnie's summer whale-oil boomtown.
ports.bakeRisk: 79.7°N — far above the 66°N ice-cap seal; reaching it IS the point, so it needs a summer-season mask exception carved along the Norwegian coast. The costliest single bake on this list; consider representing the fishery as a seasonal lane terminus at the mask edge instead.

lanes.note: Summer season only (jja) if the mask exception lands.

ports.name: Kaffa (Feodosia)
ports.significance: The Crimean Khanate's great port — grain, salt, and the Black Sea slave trade that fed the Ottoman world.
ports.bakeRisk: The Bosporus and Kerch straits are sub-cell chokepoints on a 1° grid — almost certainly needs hand-carved channel cells before any Black Sea routing works.
ports.promotionRule: Kaffa's slave market must reuse the sober human-trafficking framing (cargo.enslaved-people pattern) if represented at all. The current Middle-Passage invariant is Atlantic-specific by design; generalizing it is a deliberate decision to make at promotion, never a default. The staged lanes below carry only the licit goods.

ports.name: Istanbul (Constantinople)
ports.significance: The largest city of Europe and the Near East, provisioned by sea for the whole period — by any physical measure among the great ports of these centuries. Added at PLAN-3 R1 as the exemplar of the declared boundary: the European commercial record cannot RANK it, so it is present here, unranked, rather than silently absent.
ports.bakeRisk: Shares Kaffa's Bosporus/Kerch sub-cell chokepoint problem — one hand-carved channel job unlocks both (and the Black Sea generally).

lanes.note: The capital's Black Sea grain lifeline. Kaffa's promotionRule (sober framing for any represented coerced flow) applies to this pairing too.

ports.name: Sitka (Novo-Arkhangelsk)
ports.significance: The Russian-American Company's capital — sea-otter pelts for the Canton market, the fur rush that carried Russia to America.
ports.bakeRisk: Longitude −135° lies outside the current render bounds (−100..150) and the Canton great-circle crosses the antimeridian — needs a render-bounds extension plus a field-coverage check across the Pacific. Movement fields are global, so it is feasible; it is the display that must stretch.
